title: Caching Required: Low Latency
description: Policy pattern that enforces caching when P99 latency target is 100ms or below — the threshold at which direct database queries under any meaningful load are unlikely to reliably satisfy the SLO without a cache layer in front.

cost.costNotes: Meta-policy pattern with no direct cost. Actual caching infrastructure costs are accounted for in the cache strategy pattern selected downstream (cache-aside, write-through-cache).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Zero cost — meta-policy pattern only. Caching infrastructure costs are in cache-aside, write-through-cache, and write-back-cache patterns.
provenance.adoptionCost: Zero adoption cost — this pattern auto-detects P99 latency requirements from the spec and enforces caching as a hard compilation requirement.
provenance.licenseCost: No license cost — policy enforcement pattern only.

provides.reasoning: Detects when P99 latency target is 100ms or below — the threshold where direct database queries under production load reliably fail the SLO — and enforces /constraints/features/caching: true as a hard compilation gate.

requires.reasoning: At P99 <= 100ms, a cache layer is required to serve the majority of reads from memory and avoid the tail latency introduced by database round-trips under load.

availability.reason: General architectural enforcement pattern applicable to any low-latency system requirement

supports_nfr.reason: Pattern activates when P99 latency target is 100ms or below — the threshold at which direct database queries under production load are unlikely to reliably meet the SLO without a cache layer.
Pattern is compatible with any authentication mechanism; cache key design handles auth context.
Pattern is compatible with both audited and non-audited environments.
Caching supports all tenant isolation strategies through tenant-scoped cache key namespacing.
Pattern is compatible with both PII and non-PII workloads; PII requires cache encryption and appropriate TTL policies.
Compatible with GDPR when cache invalidation covers right-to-be-forgotten requirements.
Compatible with HIPAA when cache stores use encryption at rest and in transit.
Compatible with CCPA when cache invalidation handles data deletion requests.
Compatible with SOX; database remains the authoritative audit trail.
Consistency enforcement is delegated to the downstream cache strategy pattern.
Durability enforcement is delegated to the downstream cache strategy pattern.

supports_constraints.reason: Pattern is cloud-agnostic; in-memory caching infrastructure exists on all major clouds and on-premises.
Pattern is language-agnostic; cache client libraries exist for all major languages.
Low P99 latency targets can be required by any platform; caching enforcement is platform-agnostic.
Latency-driven caching is independent of async messaging infrastructure.
Low-latency requirements are common in AI inference serving; caching is frequently used to serve repeated prompts from memory.
Caching supports both single-tenant and multi-tenant architectures.
Low latency requirements can exist in both real-time and batch workloads.
Latency-driven caching is compatible with distributed transaction systems.
Real-time streaming workloads frequently require sub-100ms P99 latency and benefit from caching hot data.
Latency-driven caching can cache vector search results to meet tight P99 targets.
Latency-driven caching works with document stores as the backing database.
Latency-driven caching works with key-value stores as the backing database.
Latency-driven caching can cache frequently-accessed graph query results.
Latency-driven caching can cache time-series aggregations or recent data points for low-latency dashboards.
Low P99 latency requirements are especially common in OLTP workloads serving user-facing APIs.
Latency-driven caching can cache OLAP query results for low-latency analytical dashboards.

requires_constraints.reason: At P99 <= 100ms, caching is mandatory to keep tail latency within the SLO. Set constraints.features.caching: true

requires_nfr.reason: Caching improves availability by reducing DB load, but the combined cache+DB stack is bounded at four nines without multi-region active-active infrastructure.
Caching adds a network hop; p95 floor is ~1ms for in-memory cache hits.
Cache misses fall back to the database, adding tail latency; p99 floor is ~5ms with a warm cache.
Cache failures trigger immediate fallback to database; near-zero RTO is achievable.
Cache is ephemeral; database is the source of truth. RPO = 0 is achievable.
Caching infrastructure supports up to 1M req/s (3.6B/hour) with a properly sized cache cluster.
